coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of carbon dots from taurine as bioimaging agent and nanohybrid with ceria for antioxidant and antibacterial applications
coredata.dc:creator: Sharma, Anirudh
Kundu, Mousumi
Ghosh, Noyel
Chatterjee, Sharmistha
Tejwan, Neeraj
Singh, Th. Abhishek
Pabbathi, Ashok
Das, Joydeep
Sil, Parames C.
coredata.dc:description: Here we have synthesized water soluble and biocompatible carbon dots (CDs) from taurine via thermal decomposition method. The CDs showed nearly spherical shape with diameter less than 10 nm. The CDs exhibited excitation dependent fluorescence emission and could be used for mammalian cell imaging. The CDs showed excellent DPPH and hydrogen peroxide radical scavenging activity in cell free system. Besides, the CDs also displayed significant intracellular radical scavenging activity in human normal kidney epithelial (NKE) cells. Furthermore, nanohybrids consisting of both CDs and nanoceria (CeO2) were prepared and tested for their biomedical applications. The nanohybrids showed significant antioxidant activities in both cell free and intracellular conditions. The CDs and nanohybrids possessed very little toxicity upto the concentration of 100 μg/mL when treated for 24 hours in human NKE cells. The CDs as well as nanohybrids further displayed significant bacterial growth inhibition against both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria under dark as well as light illumination condition via the bacterial membrane damage. However, under the light illumination, the bacterial growth inhibition of CDs and nanohybrids was further enhanced due to the generation of reactive oxygen radicals and subsequent DNA degradation. A higher dose-dependent intracellular antioxidant and antibacterial activities of the nanohybrid is attributed to the synergistic effect of nanoceria and CDs. All these results clearly reflected that our synthesized CDs and their nanohybrids can be used for several biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Antioxidant
Antibacterial
Bioimaging
Carbon dots
Nanoceria
Reactive oxygen species

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement of intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) in Human normal kidney epithelial (NKE) cells
Synthesis and characterization of CeO2, CDs and CDs-CeO2 nanohybrid
Bacterial cell membrane potential and membrane permeability

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic representation of the synthesis of CDs, CeO2 and CDs-CeO2.

TEM images of (a) CDs, (b) CDs-CeO2 nanohybrids. Red circles indicate the CDs-CeO2 hybrids & yellow circles indicate CDs. (c) DLS analysis of CDs, CeO2 and CDs-CeO2 nanohybrid. (d) EDS analysis of CDs-CeO2 nanohybrid.
(a) FTIR spectra of taurine, CDs, CeO2, and CDs-CeO2 nanohybrid. (b) Raman spectra of CDs-CeO2 nanohybrid.

(a) UV-Visible spectra of taurine, CDs, CeO2, and CDs-CeO2 nanohybrid . (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (c) Fluorescence microscopy images of CDs. (d) Fluorescence microscopy images of MCF-7 cells after CDs uptake.
(a) H2O2 radical scavenging assay. (b) DPPH radical scavenging assay. © Intracellular ROS scavenging in human normal kidney epithelial (NKE) cells. (d) Cell viability assay in NKE cells.

(a) & (b) antibacterial activity against S. Aureus and E. Coli bacteria respectively (d: dark, l: light). (c) & (d) Gel electrophoresis assay of DNA isolated from S. aureus and E. coli bacteria respectively.
(a) & (b) Intracellular ROS levels in S. Aureus and E. coli bacteria respectively. (c) & (d) PI fluorescence and (e) & (f) DiOC6 fluorescence in S. aureus and E. coli bacteria respectively.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of carbon dots from taurine as bioimaging agent and nanohybrid with ceria for antioxidant and antibacterial applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Advanced Chemical Sciences, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Shoolini University, Solan, Himachal Pradesh, 173212, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Advanced Chemical Sciences, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Shoolini University, Solan, Himachal Pradesh, 173212, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Molecular Medicine, Bose Institute, P-1/12, CIT Scheme VII M, Kolkata, 700054, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDivision of Molecular Medicine, Bose Institute, P-1/12, CIT Scheme VII M, Kolkata, 700054, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Industrial Chemistry, School of Physical Sciences, Mizoram University, Mizoram, Aizawl, 796004, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDepartment of Industrial Chemistry, School of Physical Sciences, Mizoram University, Mizoram, Aizawl, 796004, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Physical Sciences, Mizoram University, Aizawl, 796004, Mizoram, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dDepartment of Chemistry, Physical Sciences, Mizoram University, Aizawl, 796004, Mizoram, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here we have synthesized water soluble and biocompatible carbon dots (CDs) from taurine via thermal decomposition method. The CDs showed nearly spherical shape with diameter less than 10 nm. The CDs exhibited excitation dependent fluorescence emission and could be used for mammalian cell imaging. The CDs showed excellent DPPH and hydrogen peroxide radical scavenging activity in cell free system. Besides, the CDs also displayed significant intracellular radical scavenging activity in human normal kidney epithelial (NKE) cells. Furthermore, nanohybrids consisting of both CDs and nanoceria (CeO2) were prepared and tested for their biomedical applications. The nanohybrids showed significant antioxidant activities in both cell free and intracellular conditions. The CDs and nanohybrids possessed very little toxicity upto the concentration of 100 μg/mL when treated for 24 hours in human NKE cells. The CDs as well as nanohybrids further displayed significant bacterial growth inhibition against both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria under dark as well as light illumination condition via the bacterial membrane damage. However, under the light illumination, the bacterial growth inhibition of CDs and nanohybrids was further enhanced due to the generation of reactive oxygen radicals and subsequent DNA degradation. A higher dose-dependent intracellular antioxidant and antibacterial activities of the nanohybrid is attributed to the synergistic effect of nanoceria and CDs. All these results clearly reflected that our synthesized CDs and their nanohybrids can be used for several biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are the nano-sized particles of carbon materials having diameter less than 10 nm. They have received a vast attention of the researchers because of their unique properties, such as very small size, easy cellular internalization, low toxicity, high water solubility and tunable fluorescence nature. Fluorescent CDs can be used as the bioimaging agents especially for the bacterial and cancer cells [1].

Mainly two methodologies are adopted for the synthesis of CDs: (1) top down (breaking down of larger carbon structures), and (2) bottom up (accumulation of smaller carbon structures) [1]. Various renewable and non-renewable precursors such as, glucose, orange juice, gum arabic, commercial food [2–4], amino acids [5], phenyl boronic acid [6], ethylene diamine and citric acid [7], etc. have been used for the preparation of CDs. Among those, amino acids are the most promising precursors of CDs due to their profound chemical and medicinal properties. Amino acids-derived CDs have been used in various applications, like antibacterial, antioxidant, bioimaging, drug delivery, and sensing agents [1,6]. Taurine (2-aminoethane sulfonic acid) is a derivative of cystine and methionine. It is a non-protein amino acid found in animal tissues including muscles [8–10]. Taurine can be used in the various biological applications, such as antioxidant [11], antibacterial [12], and membrane stabilizing agent [13], maintain calcium haemostasis [14], induce growth modulation [15], improve vision [16] etc. in mammalian systems. In addition, it can also be used in various other applications, such as gold recovery from water [17], chemical sensing [18], chemo selective oxidation of sulphides [19] etc. Till date, there is no report on taurine-derived CDs and their biomedical applications. Furthermore, taurine is cheap and commercially available, therefore suitable for the large-scale production of CDs.

Besides, recently researchers have also showed that the nanohybrid consisting of both CDs and several metal oxides enhanced their biological responses in a combinatorial or synergistic manner [20–22]. Several metal oxides, such as ZnO, TiO2, Bi4O5Br2, CeO2, MgO, CeO2, CuO and Cu2O show potential biomedical applications because of their low toxicity, antioxidant, antibacterial and anticancer activities [22]. Among those, CeO2 (nanoceria) is considered as one of the potential candidates; gaining much attention of researchers due to its profound antioxidant, antibacterial, antidiabetic and anticancer nature, as well as its relatively non-toxic profile [23].

Since both nanoceria and several reported CDs have potential antioxidant and antibacterial activities, we have hypothesized that the combination of nanoceria and taurine-derived CDs would be highly effective in showing their antioxidant and antibacterial effects in a combinatorial or synergistic manner. In the present study, we have first synthesized a biocompatible and water soluble CDs from taurine via thermal decomposition method. After that we have used the fluorescent CDs as a bioimaging agent. Finally, a nanohybrid made up of both the taurine-derived CDs and nanoceria were prepared and their antioxidant (both in cell free and intracellular conditions) and antibacterial (against both gram positive and gram negative bacteria) activities were compared.

Taurine and hydrogen peroxide were purchased from “Loba Chemie” Pvt. Ltd. (Maharashtra, India), while ammonium ceric nitrate (98%) was obtained from “CDH” Pvt. Ltd. (New Delhi, India). Sodium hydroxide (98%) was purchased from “RANKEM” RFCL Ltd. (Thane, Maharashtra). 3,3’-dihexyloxacarbocyanine iodide (DiOC6) dye, propidium iodide (PI) and 2’, 7’-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (H2DCFDA probe) were purchased from Merck (Maharashtra, India). Other regents like potassium phosphate salts were purchased from Central Drug House Pvt. Ltd. (New Delhi, India).

CDs were synthesized via thermal decomposition technique following the methods of Cruz et al. [24]. In brief, 4 g of taurine was taken in a 50 mL crucible and heated in a muffle furnace (Laby instruments industry, Ambala Cantt, India) at 335 °C for 14 minutes. After cooling down to room temperature, the black coloured compound was transferred to a 200 mL beaker, suspended in distilled water, and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 minutes to remove water insoluble parts. The obtained black coloured solution was lyophilized in a lyophilizer (Allied Frost, New Delhi, India) to get dry CDs powder (3.630 gm).

Nanoceria (CeO2) was synthesized via the similar method [24]. In brief, 4.38 g of ammonium ceric nitrate was first dissolved in 5 mL distilled water followed by addition of an aqueous solution of NaOH (1.2 g of NaOH dissolved in 31.5 mL distilled water) in a drop wise manner to get yellow precipitates. The yellow cerium hydroxide precipitates were washed 3 times with distilled water to remove the water soluble ion impurities and transferred to a 50 mL crucible. Subsequently, the crucible containing precipitates were heated in muffle furnace at 350°C for 15 minutes. After cooling down to room temperature, the yellow coloured nanoceria powder was obtained (1.40 g).

The nanohybrid (CDs-CeO2) was prepared following the same procedure [24]. At first 4.38 g of ammonium ceric nitrate was first dissolved in 5 mL water and NaOH solution was added to it in a drop wise manner to get yellow precipitates. The yellow cerium hydroxide precipitates were washed 3 times with distilled water to remove the water soluble ion impurities. Then the aqueous solution of 2 g of taurine was added to precipitates of cerium hydroxide and mixed thoroughly. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to 50 mL crucible and heated for 10 minutes at 350°C in a muffle furnace. The as formed brown coloured powder was suspended in distilled water, washed 3 times with water and centrifuged at 4500 rpm for 10 minutes to remove the free CDs. The resultant nanohybrid was dried in oven and weighed (0.821g).

Transmission electron microscopic techniques (FEI; FP-5022 Technai G20s-Twin, USA) was used to check the morphology and size of the synthesized nanoparticles. Elemental analysis has been done via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy technique (XPS, PHI 5000 Versa Prob II, FEI Inc. analyser). Raman spectroscopy was performed by using Invia Raman instrument made by Renishaw company (UK). Fourier transform infrared spectra were recorded using FTIR spectrometer (Agilent technologies, L1600312: CA, USA). The UV- vis absorption values were recorded using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Wensar: LMSP-UV1900; Chennai, India) and the fluorescence spectra were recorded using a spectrofluorimeter (Varioskan LUX 3020-563, Waltham, USA). The hydrodynamic size was measured by Dynamic Light Scattering instrument (Delsa, Nano C particle analyser; CA, USA). Nikon fluorescence microscope was used to capture the fluorescent images of the CDs.

Human breast cancer cell lines (MCF-7) were used for checking intracellular CDs uptake. The MCF-7 cells were first seeded on sterile glass cover slip and treated with 50 μg/mL solution of CDs for 6 hours. The fluorescence images were taken using the FTIC and DAPI filters by the fluorescent microscope.

The H2O2 scavenging activity of the as synthesized nanoparticles was investigated following the method of Ruch et al. [25] with minor modification. In brief, 43 mM H2O2 solution was prepared in 1 M phosphate buffer (pH=7.4) and various concentrations of taurine, CDs, CeO2 and CDs-CeO2 nanohybrid (ranging from 0-350 μg/mL) were mixed with 0.6 mL of the as prepared H2O2 solution in the different test tubes. After the incubation of 10 minutes at 37 °C under dark, the absorbance was measured at 230 nm. The phosphate buffer containing only H2O2 was taken as blank.
%
inhibition activity
=
(
A blank
−
A sample
/
A blank
)
*
100

The DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) scavenging ability of the nanoparticles has been investigated by the protocol as reported by Shimada et al. with some modifications [26]. In brief, we prepared the stock solution of DPPH (2mg/100 mL) in ethanol and kept in dark condition at 4 °C until its use. Aqueous solutions of CDs, CeO2, taurine, and CDs-CeO2 with different concentrations (0 to 100 μg/mL) were added to a fixed volume of 2 DPPH solution followed by incubation at 37 °C for 30 minutes in dark. Optical absorbance values were recorded at 517 nm.
% inhibition activity =
(
A
control
−
A
sample
)
/
(
A
control
)
x
100 %

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) in Human normal kidney epithelial (NKE) cells
ce:section.ce:para: In order to check the intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) level, first 2.5 × 105 cells were plated per well and treated with 50 μg/mL of CDs, CeO2, taurine and CDs-CeO2 for 12 hours. After that tertiary butyl hydroperoxide (TBHP) was added to the cells and further incubated for another 1 hour. Finally, the cells were scrapped and centrifuged at the speed of 300Xg for 5 minutes at room temperature. The cell pellet was resuspended in PBS and incubated with (2’, 7’-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate) H2DCFDA to the final concentration of 2 μM and then incubated for 25 minutes in dark at 37 °C. Finally, the fluorescence intensity was recorded via BD-FACS Calibur Flow Cytometry System with green channel.

Herein, the NKE cells were first plated in a 96-well plate (1.5 × 104 cells/well) and incubated under 5% CO2 at 37o C inside an incubator for 24 hours. Then the cells were exposed to taurine, CDs, CeO2 and CDs-CeO2 nanohybrid at various concentrations ranging from 10 to 100 μg/mL for 24 hours. After that 100 μL of MTT solution (0.5 mg/mL) were added to each well and incubated for another 4 hours. Finally, 100 μL of DMSO was added in each well to dissolve the formazan crystals and the absorbance was measured at 570 nm. Herein, the cell viability (%) was expressed with respect to the control [27].
Cell viability
( % )
=
(
A sample
/
A control
)
* 100
Here, Asample and Acontrol are the absorbance of sample andcontrol respectively.

Both gram positive (S. aureus) as well as gram negative (E. coli) bacteria have been employed to determine the growth inhibition potentials of CDs, CeO2, taurine and CDs-CeO2 nanohybrid [28]. In brief, freshly prepared bacterial suspensions in Luria–Bertani (LB) media were exposed to various concentrations (10-100 μg/mL) of taurine, CDs, CeO2 and CDs-CeO2nanohybrid for 18 hours at 37°C in a shaker. In the light illumination experiments, 36-watt LED bulb was used as light source kept 15 cm away from bacterial sample. Here, LB media with only bacteria is considered as control and LB with only nanoparticles is considered as blank. Finally, the bacterial growth was measured at 600 nm by a spectrophotometer.

Both the bacterial strains (E. coli and S. aureus) were treated with 100 μg mL−1 of taurine, CDs, CeO2, and CDs-CeO2 for 18 hours at 37 °C. DNA isolation from bacterial strains was performed by using Bacterial DNA extraction kit (Qiagen, Germany), ran on 1 % agarose gel at 110 V and bands were visualized by ethidium bromide staining [29].

In order to check the bacterial ROS level, the bacterial cells (approx. 2 × 105) were treated with taurine, CeO2, CDs and CDs-CeO2 at a concentration of 100 μg/mL for 18 hours under dark or light illuminating condition. Thereafter the cells were washed, and incubated with H2DCFDA (2 μM) in PBS at 37 °C for 25 minutes. The fluorescence intensity was measured by using a spectrofluorimeter at Ex: 495 nm and Em: 525 nm.

The cell membrane potential and permeability were measured by using (3, 3’-dihexyloxacarbocyanine) DiOC6 andpropidium iodide (PI) [30]. Bacterial cells were treated with 100 μg mL−1 of taurine, CDs, CeO2 and CDs-CeO2 nanohybrid for 18 hours at 37 °C. Thereafter the cells were washed, resuspended in PBS, and incubated with 20 μM of DiOC6 and 9 μM of PI at 37 °C, respectively [31]. Finally, red and green fluorescence of bacterial cells was examined using a spectroflurimeter at Ex/ Em = 535/617 nm and Ex/ Em = 495/525 nm, respectively [32].
All the experiments were performed at least 3 times and we expressed the results as mean ± SD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CeO2, CDs and CDs-CeO2 nanohybrid
ce:section.ce:para: Here we have reported the synthesis of CDs from taurine; nanoceria from ammonium ceric nitrate and a nanohybrid composed of CDs and nanoceria, and compared their antioxidant and antibacterial activities (Fig. 1 ). The morphology and size of CDs and CDs-CeO2 nanohybrid were checked by TEM (Fig. 2 a, b) analysis. The CDs were nearly spherical with size less than 10 nm. The CDs-CeO2 nanohybrids showed the existence of both CDs (marked with yellow circles) and CeO2 nanoparticles (marked with red circles) with irregular morphologies and size in the range of 20 - 50 nm. Besides, we have also checked their hydrodynamic size from dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiments (Fig. 2c). The hydrodynamic diameters of CDs, CeO2 and nanohybrid were found to be 13.57, 142.5 and 282.6 nm respectively, and this clearly reflects the difference in their surface nature and degree of hydration. EDS analysis displayed the existence of C, N, O, S, and Ce in the nanohybrid (Fig. 2d). The FTIR spectra of taurine, CDs, CeO2 and CDs-CeO2 nanohybrid are shown in Fig. 3 a. CDs and taurine both showed several characteristic transmittance peaks at 3196, 3036, 2968, 1614, 1508, 1205, 1174, 1034, 734, 594 and 510 cm−1. CeO2 showed a peak at 484 cm−1. Peaks at 1303,1456 and 1626 cm−1 as well as a band centred at 3355 cm−1 were also observed. Raman spectra of the nanohybrid displayed the characteristic peaks at 461 cm−1for CeO2, and at 1389 cm−1 and 1589 cm−1for the CDs (Fig. 3b). The results showed the coexistence of CeO2 and CDs in the nanohybrid.

The UV-Vis spectra of taurine, CDs, CeO2 and nanohybrid are shown in Fig. 4 a. CDs displayed four absorption peaks centred at 275, 340, 375, and 450 nm, and pure taurine showed a very weak absorption peak at 215 nm. CeO2 nanoparticles showed a characteristic broad band centred at 330 nm. In the nanohybrid the peaks were observed at 255, 340, and 375 nm with high absorption values. The CDs showed an excitation-dependent fluorescence emission nature (Fig. 4b) when excited with the wavelengths ranging from 320 to 440 nm. However, CDs showed maximum emission near 450-500 nm after excitation at 360 nm. The fluorescentt images CDs displayed blue, green as well as red colors when excited at their respective excitation wavelengths (Fig. 4c).

We have checked the possible use of the CDs as a bioimaging agent using mammalian cells via fluorescence microscopic analysis. We observed that MCF-7 cells fluoresced intense green colour upon treatment with CDs (Fig. 4d), thereby indicating the probable use of the as synthesized CDs as bioimaging agents.

H2O2 is a highly reactive molecule causing oxidative stress in cells. Here we have checked the peroxide radical scavenging activity of taurine and the other nanoparticles. Taurine, the taurine-derived CDs, nanoceria and the hybrid nanoparticle, all showed a concentration-dependent radical scavenging activity (Fig. 5 a). CDs and nanoceria showed ∼ 100% scavenging activity at concentrations ranging from 250-350 μg/mL, whereas the nanohybrid showed 60 to 82% scavenging activity within the similar concentrations range.

All the prepared samples showed concentration dependent DPPH scavenging activity (Fig. 5b). CDs showed highest 82.55 % scavenging activity at a concentration of 100 μg/mL; whereas for the nanohybrid a maximum ∼ 58 % scavenging was observed at the same concentration. The order of DPPH scavenging activity of the different samples is as follows: CDs > CDs-CeO2 > CeO2 > Taurine.

Intracellular ROS scavenging activity of taurine, taurine-derived CDs, nanoceria and the hybrid nanoparticles was checked by FACS analysis in human normal kidney epithelial (NKE) cells. The cells were first pre-treated with the respective formulations at a dose of 50 μg/mL for 12 hours followed by exposure to a ROS inducer, TBHP for 1 hour. After the exposure to TBHP, cells showed increased green fluorescence or ROS level (Fig. 5c). Pre-treatment with all the above formulations decreased the ROS levels as compared with the TBHP exposed groups, and the nanohybrid-treated group showed the maximum ROS-lowering affect among the groups.

We have further checked the cytotoxicity of all the formulations in NKE cells treated within the concentration range of 10-100 μg/mL for 24 hours. We have observed that all the formulations exerted very low toxicity, and more than 85% cells were viable upto the concentration of 100 μg/mL (Fig. 5d).

We have also checked the antibacterial activities of the synthesized nanoparticles against both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria under visible light exposure as well as dark conditions (Fig. 6 a, b). We observed that taurine, nanoceria, CDs and nanohybrid exhibited a dose-dependent bacterial growth inhibition against both the bacterial strains under dark as well as light illuminating conditions. Taurine alone could not significantly inhibit the bacterial growth under dark as well as light exposure. Besides, taurine-derived CDs were unable to show significant bacterial growth inhibition under dark, but the nanohybrid exhibited a synergistic bacterial growth inhibition (Fig. 6a & b). Moreover, the antibacterial activity of CDs, and CDs-CeO2 nanohybrid were substantially increased under visible light irradiation.

The MIC values have also been measured. Under dark, the values are 373.123, 222.319, 308.832, and 175.848μg/mL towards the gram positive strain and 489.33, 261.325, 372.904, and 163.042μg/mL towards gram negative strain for Taurine, CeO2, CDs, and CDs-CeO2 treated groups respectively. Similarly; Taurine, CeO2, CDs, and CDs-CeO2 showed MIC values of 332.378, 183.853, 146.547 and 108.592 μg/mL towards gram positive, and 413.611, 216.562, 168.197 and 137.130 μg/mL for gram negative bacteria respectively under light illuminating condition.

After that we have checked the DNA integrity in both E. coli (gram negative) and S. aureus (gram positive) bacteria under the exposure of light since the antibacterial activities of CeO2, CDs, and CDs-CeO2 were increased under light illuminating condition. The results revealed that CDs-CeO2 nanohybrid exhibited maximum degree of DNA degradation among all the treatment groups (Fig. 6c, d).

We have also checked the ability of the nanomaterials to induce oxidative stress in both bacterial strains. We observed that nanoceria, CDs and the nanohybrids significantly enhanced the ROS levels in bacteria under light irradiation as compared to dark (Fig. 7 a, b). Nanoceria, CDs and the nanohybrid caused 4.42, 7.79 and 9.54-fold increase in ROS levels in gram positive strains, whereas 3.68, 6.79 and 7.48-folds increase in ROS levels in gram-negative bacteria were found under light exposure. However, taurine did not show any significant change in ROS generation ability under dark and light illuminating conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacterial cell membrane potential and membrane permeability
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial activity of taurine, CDs, CeO2 and CDs-CeO2 nanohybrid has been finally examined by evaluating cell membrane dysfunction in both E. coli and S. aureus bacteria. The results revealed that CeO2, CDs and CDs-CeO2 nanohybrid increased membrane permeability since all these treatment groups showed increased PI uptake by the cells (Fig. 7c, d). Besides; CeO2, CDs and CDs-CeO2 treatment groups also triggered membrane depolarization since all the treatment groups exhibited decreased green fluorescence of DiOC6 by the cells (Fig. 7e, f). CDs-CeO2 nanohybrid exhibited maximum membrane dysfunction in both the type of bacteria and the damaging effect was further increased due to light exposure, whereas taurine did not show any significant change in membrane dysfunction ability under dark and light illuminating conditions.

In the present study, we have synthesized water soluble and biocompatible CDs from a conditionally essential amino acid, taurine via thermal decomposition method and investigated its applications as a bioimaging agent. Further the CDs were combined with nanoceria (CeO2 NPs) to form a nanohybrid with enhanced antioxidant and antibacterial activities.

The nanoparticles were characterized via TEM, DLS, EDS, and FTIR techniques. CDs exhibited nearly spherical morphology (<10 nm), whereas the CDs-CeO2 exhibited irregular (size ranging from 20 - 50 nm). However, the change in size of nanoceria and the hybrid nanoparticles was very clear from DLS measurements. Besides, the hydrodynamic diameters of nanoparticles were larger than the same collected from the TEM study due to the hydration/ solvation of the nanoparticles takes place in aqueous medium [33]. We further checked the presence of CeO2 and CDs in the nanohybrid by EDS which showed the coexistence of C, S, Ce, O, and N. In the FTIR spectra, taurine displayed transmittance peaks at 3196 as well as 3036 cm−1 because of the asymmetrical as well as symmetrical N-H bond stretching; at 2968 cm−1 because of C-H bond stretching; at 1614 as well as 1508 cm−1 because of asymmetrical and symmetrical deformation of NH2 group; at 1205, 1174 as well as 1034 cm−1 because of asymmetrical and symmetrical SO3stretching bond and C-N bond stretching respectively; at 734 cm−1 because of C-S bond stretching; and 594 as well as 510 cm−1 because of asymmetrical and symmetrical deformation of SO3 group [33–35]. We noticed that all the transmittance peaks of CDs are similar to those of taurine, thereby clearly indicating the presence of all the functional groups of taurine in CDs. CeO2 nanoparticles showed a characteristic peak at 484 cm−1 because of Ce-O vibrations. Besides, CeO2 showed broad bands near 3400, 1456 and 1303 cm−1 because of the adsorbed water and carbon dioxide respectively. [36]. However, in the FTIR spectrum of the CDs-CeO2 nanohybrid, the intensity of Ce-O stretching was diminished probably due to the interaction between Ce3+/4+ and the NH2/SO3H groups.

Raman spectra of CDs-CeO2 exhibited three different peaks centered at 461 cm−1 (associated with the F2g vibration mode of oxygen atom in CeO2), 1389 cm−1 (D-band), and 1583 cm−1 (G-band). The D and G-bands are related to the vibration mode of carbon atom with dangling bonds and the E2g mode of graphite respectively [37].

The optical properties of the nanoparticles were analysed by using UV-vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The CDs showed three absorption peaks centred at 275 nm, appeared due to π → π* transition of the aromatic C=C/C=N sp2 domains and the peaks at 340 nm and 375 nm, appeared due to n → π* transition from C=O of C=N groups [38,39]. A very weak peak has also been observed at 450 nm due to π → π* transition from C=S groups. Pure CeO2 showed absorption band around 330 nm [40]. CDs-CeO2 exhibited the peaks at 255, 340, and 375 nm which corresponded to the CDs present within it with minor shifting from the bare CDs might be because of the interaction between CeO2 and CDs. Besides all these peaks appeared at higher absorption values due to the presence of CeO2 within the nanohybrids. From the emission spectrum of CDs under various excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 to 440 nm, we observed that CDs showed maximum emission near 450-500 nm after excitation at 360 nm. We have observed that with the increase in excitation wavelengths, there was a red shift in the fluorescence emission [41]. The CDs exhibited a quantum yield value of 7.2 % (quinine sulphate as a stranded) with no considerable change in fluorescence intensity at storage upon 90 days as well as high salt concentration [42]. Besides, CDs showed a slight increase in fluorescence value upon increasing pH from 3 to 12 [43]. The fluorescence microscopic analysis also showed multi-color fluorescence when excited at their respective excitation wavelengths. Here, we have applied the fluorescence property of CDs for bio imaging of mammalian cells. MCF-7 cells showed intense green fluorescence when incubated with CDs and observed under blue light excitation.

Nanoceria exhibit profound antioxidant activity due to its switching between Ce3+ and Ce4+ ions on its surface [44]. It also shows profound antibacterial activity via oxidative stress [45]. Besides, taurine is also acting as a potent antioxidant under severe oxidative stress conditions [46]. Taurine has also been reported to augment the antibacterial activity of gentamicin [47]. Therefore, we have hypothesized that the combination of nanoceria and taurine-derived CDs would be highly effective in showing their antioxidant and antibacterial effects in a combinatorial or synergistic manner. To prove our hypothesis, we have checked and compared the antioxidant and antibacterial effects of the free taurine, taurine-derived CDs, nanoceria and the nanohybrid. First, we have compared the free radical scavenging activities among free taurine, CDs, nanoceria and the nanohybrid in both cell free systems and intracellular conditions. Here, first we have checked the H2O2 and DPPH scavenging activities in cell free system among the groups, and all the groups showed a concentration-dependent radical scavenging activity. CDs showed higher scavenging or reducing ability compared with taurine due to its π electron reservoir [1]. However, the nanohybrid showed little less scavenging activity in comparison to nanoceria and CDs might be due to the lesser availability of surface-active sites. Results of the intracellular ROS scavenging activities among the groups showed higher scavenging activity of CDs as compared with taurine and nanoceria. However, the higher intracellular ROS scavenging activity of the nanohybrid can be attributed to the difference in treatment time. The nanohybrid might be dissociated in intracellular conditions when treated for 12 hours, thereby releasing free CDs and CeO2 with large surface area. The cytotoxicity of all the nanoformulations was checked in NKE cells in order to find their biocompatibility. We observed that all the nanoformulations exerted very low toxicity, and more than 85 % cells were viable upto the concentration of 100 μg/mL after the treatment for 24 hours.

Finally, we have explored the antibacterial potentials of the taurine, CDs, CeO2, and the CDs-CeO2 nanohybrid against both gram positive (S. Aureus) and gram negative (E. Coli) bacteria under dark as well as light exposure. The nanohybrid showed highest bacterial growth inhibition in a synergistic manner among the groups and the antibacterial activity was further increased upon light exposure. Besides, CDs also exhibited higher antibacterial effect under light illuminating condition, whereas taurine did not show significant change under dark and light illuminating conditions. Both CDs and CDs-CeO2 nanohybrid caused extensive DNA degradation in both bacterial strains under light. No DNA laddering was observed for CDs and CDs-CeO2 nanohybrid treated samples, which is attributed to the removal of smaller DNA fragments from the gel.

Further, we have checked the intracellular ROS levels and membrane dysfunction as a possible mechanism of antibacterial effects of nanoceria and CDs [27,48-50]. The ROS generating as well as membrane damaging ability of nanoceria in bacterial cells have already been known [51]. Besides, the light induced ROS generating ability of CDs has also been documented by several other researchers [52,53]. CDs and CeO2 caused enhanced ROS formation inside the bacteria under light exposure, although the ROS producing ability of CeO2 under light exposure was less than CDs. In addition, CDs and CeO2 nanohybrids also caused bacterial membrane depolarization and increased membrane permeability under dark as well as light illuminating conditions. However, the nanohybrid caused highest degree of bacterial ROS formation and membrane dysfunction among the groups, thereby exerting maximum bacterial growth inhibition. Thus, we conclude that the insignificant antibacterial activity of CDs under dark condition is attributed to the physical damage of bacterial cell membrane; whereas the augmented antibacterial activity of CDs under light exposure is attributed to the high level of ROS production which further enhances cell membrane damage [54–56].

In conclusion, we have synthesized CDs from taurine via thermal decomposition method. The CDs showed blue, red and green fluorescence light emission under the UV, blue and green excitation respectively and could be used as a bio-imaging agent. The CDs showed excellent DPPH and peroxide radical scavenging activity. Further, we have combined both CDs and nanoceria to make a nanohybrid which showed enhanced antioxidant and antibacterial activities in a synergistic manner. Besides, our synthesized CDs could behave as either pro-oxidant or anti-oxidant agents depending on the presence or absence of light. Overall, the present work leads to the advancement of a water soluble and biocompatible, fluorescent CDs and its nanohybrid in the area of biomedicine with enormous prospective.
This research is funded by Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB) Start-up research grant (SRG/2019/ 000272), DST, Govt. of India.

Anirudh Sharma: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization, Data curation. Mousumi Kundu: Methodology, Investigation. Noyel Ghosh: Investigation, Methodology. Sharmistha Chatterjee: . Neeraj Tejwan: Investigation, Methodology. Th. Abhishek Singh: Methodology, Investigation. Ashok Pabbathi: Data curation. Joydeep Das: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Parames C. Sil: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no competing interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Shoolini University, Solan, H.P., India and Bose institute, Kolkata, W.B., India for providing instrumental facilities.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal-doped and hybrid carbon dots: a comprehensive review on their synthesis and biomedical applications
